item.title: I found Ariella Steinhorn @asteinhorn through her Substack, Nonlinear, where she...
item.description:
🖼️ Slideshow (12 medier)

@asteinhorn

I found Ariella Steinhorn @asteinhorn through her Substack, Nonlinear, where she writes with scroll-stopping vulnerability about power dynamics, work culture, relationships, and love.

So it’s fitting that I fell in love with her writing the way one might fall in love with a person. Her words spoke to me: poignant, poetic and peppered with common ground language tantamount to wordless chemistry between two people.

Beyond our shared interest in those “who don’t fit the usual mold or boxes,” our common ground was comms.

Like me, Ariella was paid to wield her pen, crafting media-friendly narratives and ghostwriting for the employers that hired her.

Unlike me, her brief but life-altering time at behemoths including Uber and a subsidiary of WeWork exposed her to the dark underbelly of tech overlords, who insisted on sanitized versions of truth for the sake of profit over people.

Her words were her livelihood, but also her power.

So when Ariella’s words were taken from her, twisted and defiled by high-ranking men and the systems designed to protect them, she was effectively stripped of that power.

She has since reclaimed it by amplifying voices that have been silenced – from whistleblowers at Palantir to Boeing – which she brilliantly illuminates alongside journalist Eli Rosenberg in her second Substack, Hard Reset.

It is this fearlessness in holding power accountable that struck me about Ariella when I first met her, whose petite frame and pixie-like face seem at odds with her outsized boldness.

But I quickly learned in my conversation with the fellow logophile that the real gift of giving voice to others was that she found her own.

Read on for Ariella’s wisdom on why living truthfully is non-negotiable, and trusting the world will catch you.

#hardreset #nonlinear #substack

Se slideshow på Instagram
item.content:encoded:
🖼️ Slideshow (12 medier)

@asteinhorn

I found Ariella Steinhorn @asteinhorn through her Substack, Nonlinear, where she writes with scroll-stopping vulnerability about power dynamics, work culture, relationships, and love.

So it’s fitting that I fell in love with her writing the way one might fall in love with a person. Her words spoke to me: poignant, poetic and peppered with common ground language tantamount to wordless chemistry between two people.

Beyond our shared interest in those “who don’t fit the usual mold or boxes,” our common ground was comms.

Like me, Ariella was paid to wield her pen, crafting media-friendly narratives and ghostwriting for the employers that hired her.

Unlike me, her brief but life-altering time at behemoths including Uber and a subsidiary of WeWork exposed her to the dark underbelly of tech overlords, who insisted on sanitized versions of truth for the sake of profit over people.

Her words were her livelihood, but also her power.

So when Ariella’s words were taken from her, twisted and defiled by high-ranking men and the systems designed to protect them, she was effectively stripped of that power.

She has since reclaimed it by amplifying voices that have been silenced – from whistleblowers at Palantir to Boeing – which she brilliantly illuminates alongside journalist Eli Rosenberg in her second Substack, Hard Reset.

It is this fearlessness in holding power accountable that struck me about Ariella when I first met her, whose petite frame and pixie-like face seem at odds with her outsized boldness.

But I quickly learned in my conversation with the fellow logophile that the real gift of giving voice to others was that she found her own.

Read on for Ariella’s wisdom on why living truthfully is non-negotiable, and trusting the world will catch you.

#hardreset #nonlinear #substack

Se slideshow på Instagram
item.author: asteinhorn
